_comment: SHARED TRUTH for the share rule. The miner (Rust) and the pool (Python) must decide IDENTICALLY whether a
hash meets a target. If they disagree, the miner submits work the pool throws away -- the CPU burns and
nobody is paid. Both sides read THIS file and must reproduce every verdict.
Until now each side only tested itself: the Rust test is even called 'match_pool_rule', but it never runs
the pool -- it assumes the rule. This file replaces that assumption with a check.
RandomX returns the hash in native order (little-endian). Both sides reverse it to big-endian and compare
<= against the big-endian target. 'hash_raw_le' is what RandomX hands over; 'target_be' is what the job
carries; 'accept' is the only correct answer.
Any change here must be made ONCE and reproduced by both sides. The copy in the pool is checked byte for

vectors.name: hash equals target -> accepted (the boundary is inclusive)
hash one below the target -> accepted
hash one above the target -> rejected (off-by-one on the boundary)
max hash vs max target -> equal -> accepted
max hash vs the hardest target -> rejected
THE ENDIAN TRAP: leading zeros in native order. Read the wrong way round this hash looks tiny and would be accepted; read correctly it is enormous and must be rejected.
THE MIRROR TRAP: read the wrong way round this one looks enormous and would be rejected; read correctly it is tiny and must be accepted.
the whole value packed in the most significant byte (big-endian), exactly equal to the target -> accepted

one step above the target in the most significant byte (big-endian) -> rejected. Note the 02 sits at the END of the native hash: writing it at the front (the intuitive mistake) makes the value 2, not 2^249, and the case would silently test nothing.
realistic target (network difficulty 1e0fffff style): hash genuinely under it -> accepted
realistic target: hash just over the top -> rejected
